\t\"@type\":\"CourseInstance\",\n\t\"courseMode\":\"onsite\",\n\t\"startDate\":\"2024-10-01\",\n\t\"endDate\":\"2025-07-01\",\n\t\"location\":\"Department of Computer Science, University of Oxford\",\n\t\"inLanguage\": \"en\",\n\t\"image\":{\"@type\":\"ImageObject\",\"url\":\"https://www.cs.ox.ac.uk/images/CompSci_logo_landscapeL_rgb_corrected.jpg\",\"width\":704,\"height\":241},\n\t\"courseSchedule\":\n\t{\n\t\t\"@type\": \"Schedule\",\n\t\t\"repeatCount\": 8,\n\t\t\"repeatFrequency\": \"Weekly\"\n\t}\n\t\n\t,\"courseWorkload\":\"PT48H\"\n\t\t\t\n  }\n}\n</script><div class=\"content-width\"><ol class=\"breadcrumb text-uppercase\"><li><a href=\"/\">Home</a></li><li><a href=\"/ourstudents/index.html\">Student Space</a></li><li><a href=\"/teaching/courses/\">Courses</a></li><li><span class=\"youarehere\">Quantum Processes and Computation</span></li></ol></div><div class=\"content-width space-below mobile-menu-placeholder visible-xs visible-sm\"></div><div class=\"container-fluid content-width\"><div class=\"row\"><div class=\"col-xs-12 col-md-9 col-md-push-3\" id=\"maincontent\"><!-- CONTENT (Column 2) --><div class=\"page-title\"><h1>Quantum Processes and Computation: \n        \t\t\t\t\t2024-2025</h1></div><table style=\"width: 100%\"><tr><td class=\"personInfo\" style=\"vertical-align: top; padding-right:1em\"><p><strong>Lecturer</strong></p></td><td><p class=\"listedlinks\"><a href=\"/people/Aleks.Kissinger\">Aleks Kissinger</a></p></td></tr><tr><td class=\"personInfo\" style=\"vertical-align: top; padding-right:1em\"><p><b>Degrees</b></p></td><td><p class=\"oneliner\"><a href=\"/teaching/csp/PartC\">Schedule C1 (CS&amp;P)</a> —\n<a href=\"/teaching/csp\">Computer Science and Philosophy</a></p><p class=\"oneliner\"><a href=\"/teaching/bacompsci/PartC\">Schedule C1</a> —\n<a href=\"/teaching/bacompsci\">Computer Science</a></p><p class=\"oneliner\"><a href=\"/teaching/mcs/PartC\">Schedule C1</a> —\n<a href=\"/teaching/mcs\">Mathematics and Computer Science</a></p><p class=\"oneliner\"><a href=\"http://www.maths.ox.ac.uk/members/students/postgraduate-courses/msc-mfocs\">MSc in Mathematics and Foundations of Computer Science</a></p></td></tr><tr><td class=\"personInfo\" style=\"vertical-align: top; padding-right:1em\"><p><strong>Term</strong></p></td><td><p class=\"listedlinks\">Michaelmas Term 2024\n       (24 lectures)\n    </p></td></tr></table><h2>Overview</h2>\n<p>Both physics and computer science have been very dominant scientific and technological disciplines in the previous century. Quantum Computer Science aims at combining both and may come to play a similarly important role in this century. Combining the existing expertise in both fields proves to be a non-trivial but very exciting interdisciplinary journey. Besides the actual issue of building a quantum computer or realising quantum protocols it involves a fascinating encounter of concepts and formal tools which arose in distinct disciplines. This course provides an interdisciplinary introduction to the emerging field of quantum computer science, explaining basic quantum mechanics (including finite dimensional Hilbert spaces and their tensor products), quantum entanglement, its structure and its physical consequences and introduces qubits. We introduce the basics of quantum algorithms and delve into the how to represent and reason about quantum computations using a powerful graphical tool called the ZX calculus. We will apply this tool to discuss optimisation and classical simulation of quantum computation, as well as quantum error correction and fault-tolerant quantum computing, which are key ingredients in emerging quantum technologies.</p>\n\n<ul>\n  <li><b>Lecture</b> dates/times TBC. <!-- will be held in person in the Tony Hoare Room, every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday 12:00-13:00 UK time (full timetable <b><a href=\"https://www.cs.ox.ac.uk/teaching/timetables/timetable-MT2024.html\">here</a></b>). --> <i>Recordings will appear online after each lecture.</i></li>\n  <!-- <li> -->\n  <!--   <b>Discussion</b> (annoucements, offline Q&amp;A, research questions, etc) are also on MS Teams -->\n  <!--     <a href=\"https://teams.microsoft.com/l/channel/19%3a7cb1e304c4654b598e820e4de436ed0e%40thread.tacv2/General?groupId=af33b9c4-b6c4-4b89-9b3d-8e732e74cd84&tenantId=cc95de1b-97f5-4f93-b4ba-fe68b852cf91\">here</a>. -->\n  <!-- </li> -->\n  <li><b>Class info</b>, including weekly deadlines is on <a href=\"https://www.cs.ox.ac.uk/minerva/class_lists.pl\">Minerva</a></li>\n  <li><b>Everything else</b> (problem sheets, schedule of material, etc) is here on the course website</li>\n</ul>\n\n<p><b>The course consists of:</b></p>\n\n<ul>\n  <li>24 lectures (see <b>Lectures</b> below)</li>\n  <li>6 classes (weeks 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8). Here are the <b>problem sheets</b>:\n    <ul>\n      <li><b><a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/assignment1.pdf\">Assignment 1</a></b>\n         <!-- (<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/solutions/assignment1.pdf\">solutions</a>)  -->\n      </li>\n      <li><b><a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/assignment2.pdf\">Assignment 2</a></b>\n         <!-- (<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/solutions/assignment2.pdf\">solutions</a>)  -->\n      </li>\n      <li><b><a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/assignment3.ipynb\" download>Assignment 3</a></b>\n         <!-- (<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/solutions/assignment3.ipynb\" download>solutions</a>)  -->\n      </li>\n      <li><b><a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/assignment4.pdf\">Assignment 4</a></b>\n         <!-- (<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/solutions/assignment4.pdf\">solutions</a>)  -->\n      </li>\n      <li><b><a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/assignment5.pdf\">Assignment 5</a></b>\n         <!-- (<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/solutions/assignment5.pdf\">solutions</a>)  -->\n      </li>\n      <li><b><a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/assignment6.pdf\">Assignment 6</a></b>\n         <!-- (<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/solutions/assignment6.pdf\">solutions</a>)  -->\n      </li>\n    </ul>\n    They will be made available a week before the deadline. <a href=\"https://www.cs.ox.ac.uk/minerva/class_lists.pl\">Click here</a> to see which class you are in and how to submit your work.\n  </li>\n  <li><b>The miniproject</b>. This will have a similar format to the problem sheets, but questions will be much more in-depth and/or open-ended.</li>\n</ul>\n\n\n<a name=\"lecturelist\"></a>\n<h2>Lectures</h2>\n\n<p>The following is a provisional list of topics / sections covered in the lectures. This schedule is subject to change.</p>\n\n<p>Handwritten lecture notes (\n  <a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/QPC-notes-2024-1.pdf\">part 1</a>,\n  <a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/QPC-notes-2024-2.pdf\">part 2</a>,\n  <a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/QPC-notes-2024-3.pdf\">part 3</a>,\n  <a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/QPC-notes-2024-4.pdf\">part 4</a>,\n  <a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/QPC-notes-2024-5.pdf\">part 5</a>\n)\n<!--,\n<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2020/QPC-notes-2020-3.pdf\">part 3</a>,\n<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2020/QPC-notes-2020-4.pdf\">part 4</a>,\n<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2022/QPC-notes-2022-4a.pdf\">part 4a</a>,\n<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2022/QPC-notes-2022-5.pdf\">part 5</a>)--> cover the material in each lecture.</p>\n\n<style type=\"text/css\">\nul.lectures li:nth-child(2n) { margin-bottom: 1em; }\n</style>\n\n<ul class=\"lectures\">\n  <li><b>Lecture 1:</b> Introduction to diagrammatic reasoning (<a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2024/slides/qpc-lecture1.html\">slides</a>)</li>\n    <li><b>Lecture 2:</b> States and effects, string diagrams, and (non-)separability</li>\n\n    <li><b>Lecture 3:</b> Quantum-like features from string diagrams (video with Oxford login:\n      <a href=\"https://unioxfordnexus-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/scat3509_ox_ac_uk/EbWkrUbuVLRBkD0lsXbVnWkBi8PW2hNEnaI1jImyd5kbVA?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D&e=LFbm3q\">part 1</a>,\n      <a href=\"https://unioxfordnexus-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/scat3509_ox_ac_uk/EWvLv_66CsZIhZ_gMZq3ifMBb-I5_eG_-LU-u4UBqjf8tQ?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D&e=ZRYpwc\">part 2</a>)\n    </li>\n    <li><b>Lecture 4:</b> Bases, sums, and Hilbert spaces (video with Oxfored login:\n      <a href=\"https://unioxfordnexus-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/scat3509_ox_ac_uk/EYgNaj4PgARJncMaVg_LOU4BOj44TPvgO6eVON9rwDG41g?e=EjqKKE&nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D\">video</a>)</li>\n\n    <li><b>Lecture 5:</b> Quantum theory is SCUM: states, compound systems, and unitaries</li>\n    <li><b>Lecture 6:</b> Measurements, the quantum circuit model, and quantum algorithms</li>\n\n    <li><b>Lecture 7:</b> Quantum algorithms and circuit problems</li>\n    <li><b>Lecture 8:</b> The ZX-calculus</li>\n\n    <li><b>Lecture 9:</b> Phase-free ZX and CNOT circuits</li>\n    <li><b>Lecture 10:</b> Clifford circuits and diagrams</li>\n\n    <li><b>Lecture 11:</b> Simplifying Clifford circuits and applications</li>\n    <li><b>Lecture 12:</b> Phase gadgets and optimisation</li>\n\n    <li><b>Lecture 13:</b> Pauli gadgets and quantum simulation</li>\n    <li><b>Lecture 14:</b> Stabiliser theory and quantum error correction</li>\n\n    <li><b>Lecture 15:</b> Interlude: quantum algorithms and complexity (guest lecturer Matty Hoban)</li>\n    <li><b>Lecture 16:</b> CSS codes and fault-tolerant quantum computation</li>\n</ul>\n\n<!--\n<a name=\"practical\"></a>\n<h2>Practical</h2>\n\n<p>The practical for this course will be in 2 parts. You are free to discuss either part with your fellow students, your teacher, on Teams etc, and to work in groups. I only ask that you not spoil the problems for other students by sharing full solutions <i>en masse</i>.</p>\n\n<ul>\n    <li><b><a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2021/QPC%20Practical%201.ipynb\" download>Part 1</a></b></li>\n    <li><b><a href=\"/people/aleks.kissinger/courses/qpc2021/QPC%20Practical%202.ipynb\" download>Part 2</a></b></li>\n</ul>\n\n<p>Click the links above to download Part 1 and Part 2 (note: you may need to right click the link and click \"Save As\").</p>\n\n<p>The practicals are given as <a href=\"https://jupyter.org/\">Jupyter</a> notebooks. There are (at least) two ways to complete this practical, depending on whether you want to install some software on your own computer or just do everything in a browser.</p>\n\n<ul>\n    <li><b>Local option:</b> install Python 3, Jupyter, and the required libraries (<code>numpy</code>, <code>pyzx</code>, and <code>qiskit</code>) on your computer. <a href=\"https://www.anaconda.com/\">Anaconda</a> is a popular way to install everything at once. It is available on Windows, Mac, and Linux. For Linux, it may be easier to use your system's package manager. Once you have Python 3 and Jupyter set up, <code>pyzx</code> and <code>qiskit</code> can be installed using <a href=\"https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/\">pip</a>, possibly directly from the Jupyter notebook via the <code>%pip</code> command. Note you will not need <code>qiskit</code> until Part 2.</li>\n    <li><b>Cloud option:</b> create a free account on the <a href=\"https://quantum-computing.ibm.com/\">IBM Quantum Experience</a> and sign in with your IBMid. Click the icon for \"Quantum Lab\" in the sidebar, and click \"Import\" to upload a Jupyter notebook into cloud storage. Click on the notebook to edit it online. Once your notebook is finished, you can download it again by clicking <code>File &gt; Download As &gt; Notebook</code>. I suggest you do this regularly to avoid accidentally losing work.</li>\n</ul>\n\n<p>I've tested both of these options, and the both seem to work pretty well. You may have your own preferences (e.g. <a href=\"https://colab.research.google.com\">Google Colab</a> might work, but I haven't tried it). However, to complete Part 2, you will still need to set up a free account on the IBM Quantum Experience, even if you went with the <b>Local option</b>. This is because you will need an IBMid to set up the <a href=\"https://github.com/Qiskit/qiskit-ibmq-provider/#updating-your-ibm-q-experience-credentials\">IBM Quantum Provider</a>, which will allow cloud access to IBM's public quantum computers. This is a bit tricky to get working, so I suggest doing it in advance. If you use the <b>Cloud option</b> via IBM, this extra bit of setup is not necessary.</p>\n-->\n<h2>Learning outcomes</h2>The student will know by the end of the course what quantum computing and quantum protocols are about, why they matter, and what the scientific prospects of the field are. This includes a structural understanding of some basic quantum mechanics, knowledge of important algorithms such as Grover's and Shor's algorithm and important protocols such as quantum teleportation. </p><p>At the same time, the student will understand diagrammatic reasoning as an alternative form of mathematics.<h2>Prerequisites</h2><p>We do not assume any prior knowledge of quantum mechanics. However, a solid understanding of basic linear algebra (finite-dimensional vector spaces, matrices, eigenvectors and eigenvalues, linear maps etc.) is required as a pre-requisite. The course notes and the slides contain an overview of this material, so we advise students with a limited background in linear algebra to consult the course notes before the course starts.</p>\n<p>To do the practicals, you will need to have a basic working knowledge of the Python programming language. If you've never used Python before, I suggest working through the first 4 sections of: <a href=\"https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/\">https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/</a>.</p><h2>Synopsis</h2><p><span>[from CUP book page] The unique features of the quantum world are explained in this book through the language of diagrams, setting out an innovative visual method for presenting complex theories. Requiring only basic mathematical literacy, this book employs a unique formalism that builds an intuitive understanding of quantum features while eliminating the need for complex calculations. This entirely diagrammatic presentation of quantum theory represents the culmination of ten years of research, uniting classical techniques in linear algebra and Hilbert spaces with cutting-edge developments in quantum computation and foundations. Written in an entertaining and user-friendly style and including more than one hundred exercises, this book is an ideal first course in quantum theory, foundations, and computation for students from undergraduate to Ph.D. level, as well as an opportunity for researchers from a broad range of fields, from physics to biology, linguistics, and cognitive science, to discover a new set of tools for studying processes and interaction.</span></p><h2>Syllabus</h2><P><b>Summary / keywords:</b> diagram, parallel and sequential composition of diagrams, string diagram, transpose, adjoints and conjugate, umitarity and inner-product, Bell state, teleportation, no universal separability, no universal cloning, matrix calcu;us, Hilbert space, completeness for string diagrams, logic gate, Bell-basis, doubling, eliminating global phases, discarding, causality, Stinespirng dilation, no-signalling, Krauss decomposition and mixing, ONB measurements, entaglement swapping, gate-teleportation, Naimark dilation, von Neumann measurements, POVM measurements, tomography, classical wires, dense coding, classical-quantum map, copy, delete, measure, encode, classical, quantum and bastard spiders, phase spiders, phase group, complementarity, QKD, strong complementarity, classical subgroup, ZX-calculus, its universality and completeness for stabiliser QM, quantum non-locality, Spekkens' toy theory, circuit model of quantum computing, quantum algorithms (DJ, search, HS), MBQC, resource theories, purity theory, LOCC- and SLOCC-entanglement</P>\n\n<h2>Reading list</h2><p>The textbook for this course is&nbsp;<em>Picturing Quantum Processes: A First Course in Quantum Theory and Diagrammatic Reasoning</em>.</p>\n<p>It should be available in many libraries throughout the university and most online bookstores. The ebook PDF is also available for free to all members of Oxford University through a deal between the Bodleian Library and Cambridge University Press:</p>\n<p><a href=\"https://ezproxy-prd.bodleian.ox.ac.uk:2117/core/books/picturing-quantum-processes/1119568B3101F3A685BE832FEEC53E52\">https://ezproxy-prd.bodleian.ox.ac.uk:2117/core/books/picturing-quantum-processes/1119568B3101F3A685BE832FEEC53E52</a></p>\n<p>If the link above doesn't work, go to <a href=\"http://solo.bodleian.ox.ac.uk\">http://solo.bodleian.ox.ac.uk</a>, search for the book title, and click on the \"Online Access\" version. Under the section \"View Online\", click the link to Cambridge Core to view the book on CUP's website.</p><h2>Related research</h2><table><tr><td class=\"personInfo\"><p><b>Themes</b></p></td><td><p class=\"listedlinks\"><a href=\"/research/fls\">Quantum</a></p></td></tr></table><h2>Taking our courses</h2><p><strong>This form is not to be used by students studying for a degree in the\n\tDepartment of Computer Science, or for Visiting Students who are registered for\n\tComputer Science courses</strong></p><p>\n\tOther matriculated University of Oxford students who are interested in taking this,\n\tor other, courses in the Department of Computer Science, must complete\n\t<a href=\"https://forms.office.com/e/WRVUXCyw1q\" target=\"_blank\">this online form</a>\n\tby 17.00 on Friday of 0th week of term in which the course is taught.\n\tLate requests, and requests sent by email, will not be considered.\n\tAll requests must be approved by the relevant Computer Science departmental\n\tcommittee and can only be submitted using this form.\n\t</p></div><div class=\"col-xs-12 col-md-3 col-md-pull-9 side-menu-col\"><h2 class=\"side-menu-title text-uppercase\"><a href=\"/ourstudents/index.html\">Student Space</a></h2><ul class=\"side-menu\"><li class=\"active mobile-menu-source\"><a href=\"/teaching/courses/\">Course A-Z</a><ul><li class=\"side-menu-heading
